intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The remit evaluates the uAgents framework runtime — the Python `uagents` and `uagents-core` packages — as the default security posture handed to every agent built on it, not any single deployed agent. It is authorized to create per-agent cryptographic identity and wallet keypairs, register addresses and endpoints on the Fetch.ai Almanac, receive inbound messages over an HTTP `/submit` endpoint that it must authenticate and schema-validate before typed dispatch, and send signed outbound messages. Private keys, wallet keys and seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext; administrative, introspection, and message-history interfaces MUST NOT be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers; binding to a public interface and enabling remote inspector reachability each require operator approval; and replayed or expired signed envelopes MUST NOT be re-processed.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python framework: `uagents-core` provides the crypto layer (`Identity` over SECP256k1 with bech32 addresses, `Envelope` sign/verify) and `uagents` provides the runtime — an ASGI/uvicorn inbound server in `asgi.py`, an Almanac ledger client (`network.py`, `registration.py`, `resolver.py`), typed dispatch (`dispatch.py`), and a file-backed `KeyValueStore` (`storage/__init__.py`). The inbound server binds `HOST = "0.0.0.0"` by default, runs uvicorn with `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` and wildcard CORS, and registers agent-inspector REST endpoints (`/agent_info`, `/messages`, `/connect`, `/disconnect`) whenever `enable_agent_inspector` is left at its default `True`. Inbound envelope signatures are verified for agent senders, but the dispatch path never consults `expires` or `nonce`, and `save_private_keys()` writes identity and wallet keys to `private_keys.json` as cleartext.

behavior_summary: The framework ships genuine cryptographic primitives — enforced, unit-tested envelope signature verification and deterministic seed-derived identity — but its default network posture and key-handling contradict the remit the runtime is supposed to guarantee. The dominant pattern is a permissive default surface: the ASGI server binds to `0.0.0.0`, enables the agent inspector by default, exempts `/agent_info` from the loopback guard, and runs uvicorn with `forwarded_allow_ips="*"`, so a remote caller can spoof a loopback source and reach introspection, message-history, and Almanac (un)registration endpoints — the remit's own "spoofed loopback" bad-behavior example. Compounding this, the inbound dispatch path enforces signatures but never checks `expires` or `nonce`, so a captured signed envelope replays freely, and `save_private_keys()` persists identity and wallet keys to disk in plaintext — a directly forbidden data movement.

rules.rule_text: Executing operating-system shell commands or arbitrary code on behalf of a remote message sender.
Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form.
Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.
Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers.
Any remote counterparty whose asserted identity has not been cryptographically verified.
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.
Sensitive configuration secrets MUST NOT be embedded as literals in framework or agent source.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.
Dispatching an inbound message from a non-user agent sender whose envelope signature has not been verified.
Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.
Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.
Maximum retries before escalation: bounded; registration and delivery retries must be finite.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Halt Agent and Alert Operator
rules.rule_text: An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.

findings.summary: Default config lets a remote caller spoof a loopback source and reach the agent's introspection, message-history, and Almanac registration endpoints.
findings.description: The ASGI server binds `0.0.0.0`, enables the inspector by default, exempts `/agent_info` from the loopback guard, and runs uvicorn with `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` so `scope["client"]` is set from an attacker-supplied `X-Forwarded-For`. A remote request carrying `X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1` therefore satisfies the `"127.0.0.1" in scope["client"]` guard and reaches `/messages`, `/connect`, and `/disconnect`, while `/agent_info` needs no spoof at all. This is the remit's own "spoofed loopback source address" bad-behavior example.
policy_rule_text: Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers.
Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.

evidence.snippet: "reserved-endpoint guard checks `request_path in set(RESERVED_ENDPOINTS) - {\"/agent_info\"}` and `\"127.0.0.1\" not in scope[\"client\"]` — /agent_info exempt; guard trusts scope client which forwarded_allow_ips=\"*\" (:184) sets from X-Forwarded-For"

"enable_agent_inspector default True (:305) registers /agent_info,/messages,/connect,/disconnect on the 0.0.0.0-bound server (asgi.py:23); /messages returns message history, /connect+/disconnect drive Almanac (un)registration"

recommended_actions: Set `forwarded_allow_ips` to the specific trusted proxy IPs (never `"*"`) so `scope["client"]` cannot be spoofed, and remove the `/agent_info` exemption so all reserved endpoints share the loopback guard.
Default `enable_agent_inspector` to False and bind loopback by default (see PRAX-2026-07-17-004/005), requiring explicit operator opt-in before any admin/introspection endpoint is remotely reachable.

findings.summary: Inbound dispatch verifies the envelope signature but never checks `expires` or `nonce`, so a captured signed envelope can be replayed and re-processed.
findings.description: `ASGIServer.__call__` verifies the signature for agent senders and then dispatches, but never rejects an envelope whose `expires` timestamp is in the past and keeps no seen-nonce record, though both fields exist on the envelope and are covered by the signature. `env.expires` is consulted only to size the sync-query wait, not to reject stale messages. A recorded valid envelope therefore re-runs its handler on every resend — the remit's Never-Allowed replay/expiry case.
policy_rule_text: Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.

evidence.snippet: "after `env.verify()` the code calls dispatcher.dispatch_msg with no expiry rejection and no nonce/replay cache; env.expires used only at :390 for sync-wait timeout"
"expires and nonce are optional fields folded into _digest only when present — signed but never enforced by the receiver"

recommended_actions: In `ASGIServer.__call__`, after `env.verify()` reject any envelope whose `expires` is older than now, and maintain a bounded per-session seen-nonce set to drop replays before `dispatch_msg`.
Consider making `expires`/`nonce` mandatory on the inbound path so unsigned-freshness envelopes are rejected rather than silently accepted.

findings.summary: `save_private_keys()` writes the agent identity key and wallet key to `private_keys.json` in cleartext, the exact forbidden data movement.
findings.description: When an agent is constructed with a `name` and no seed, `get_or_create_private_keys` generates keys and calls `save_private_keys`, which serializes `identity_key` and `wallet_key` to `private_keys.json` in the working directory as plaintext JSON with no encryption and no restrictive file mode. These keys control on-chain assets and agent identity, and the remit forbids writing them to disk in plaintext. (A secondary bug at :149 persists a freshly-generated wallet key rather than the returned one.)
policy_rule_text: Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form.
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.

evidence.snippet: "save_private_keys writes {identity_key, wallet_key} via json.dump to private_keys.json in cwd, no encryption, no chmod"
"get_or_create_private_keys generates Identity/wallet keys and persists them in cleartext via save_private_keys"

recommended_actions: Stop writing raw key material to `private_keys.json`; derive keys from an operator-supplied seed held in an environment variable or a secret manager / OS keychain, or at minimum encrypt at rest and write with `0600` permissions.
Fix the `:149` wallet-key mismatch so the persisted and returned wallet keys are identical.

findings.summary: The agent HTTP server binds `0.0.0.0` (all interfaces) by default, an approval-required action taken with no operator opt-in.
findings.description: The module-level `HOST = "0.0.0.0"` is passed directly to the uvicorn config with no loopback default and no gate, so every agent is publicly reachable the moment it starts. The remit requires operator approval before binding to a non-loopback interface. This exposure of `/submit` stands on its own and is also the reachability precondition for the PRAX-2026-07-17-001 admin-endpoint chain.
policy_rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

evidence.snippet: "HOST = \"0.0.0.0\" module constant; passed as uvicorn.Config(host=HOST) at :179-181 with no loopback default or approval gate"

recommended_actions: Default `HOST` to `127.0.0.1` and require an explicit operator-set option (env var or constructor argument) to bind a public interface, logging a warning when a public bind is selected.

findings.summary: The agent inspector is enabled by default, standing up message-history and Almanac registration-control endpoints the remit classifies as restricted.
findings.description: `enable_agent_inspector` defaults to `True`, so every agent registers `/agent_info`, `/messages` (message history), `/connect` and `/disconnect` (Almanac registration and unregistration) without operator action. The remit marks these administrative/inspection endpoints as restricted and requires approval before enabling their reachability. Even independent of the public-bind chain, defaulting the surface on diverges from the intended default-off posture.
policy_rule_text: Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.

evidence.snippet: "on_rest_get('/messages') returns message history; on_rest_post('/connect')/('/disconnect') drive Almanac register/unregister, all gated only by enable_agent_inspector"

recommended_actions: Default `enable_agent_inspector` to False so the inspector, message-history, and registration-control endpoints require explicit operator opt-in.

findings.summary: Signature verification is conditional on a signature being present, so an unsigned sync response is accepted and a `user`-prefixed sender bypasses verification entirely.
findings.description: On the client path, a sync response envelope is verified only inside `if env.signature:` — an envelope with no signature skips the check and is dispatched. On the inbound path, `if not is_user_address(env.sender)` routes any sender whose address begins with `user` around `env.verify()`. The framework does mitigate by routing unverified inbound messages to separate unsigned handlers, so this is partial rather than absent, but verification is opt-out-by-omission rather than mandatory where a signature is expected.
policy_rule_text: Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.
Any remote counterparty whose asserted identity has not been cryptographically verified.
Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.

evidence.snippet: "sync response: `if env.signature:` gates verify; an unsigned response falls through to dispatch_sync_response_envelope unverified"
"`if not is_user_address(env.sender)` — a sender prefixed 'user' skips env.verify() and is dispatched with an unauthenticated, sender-asserted address"

recommended_actions: On the sync-response path, treat a missing signature as a verification failure (reject) rather than falling through to dispatch.
Pass an explicit `verified` flag to `dispatch_msg`/handlers so downstream code can never treat a `user`-prefixed or unsigned sender as authenticated for a security decision.

findings.summary: The server sends `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on every response, letting any web origin read the unauthenticated GET endpoints cross-origin.
findings.description: The uvicorn config injects wildcard CORS headers for all responses, so a malicious web page loaded in an operator's browser can issue cross-origin reads of `/agent_info` and `/messages` and cross-origin POSTs to `/submit`. `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` is `false`, which limits cookie-based escalation, but the inspector endpoints carry no auth of their own, so wildcard CORS broadens their reach.

evidence.snippet: "uvicorn headers set Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, Allow-Methods GET/POST/OPTIONS, Allow-Headers * on all responses"

recommended_actions: Restrict `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to an operator-configured allowlist (or omit CORS headers) instead of `*`, especially for the inspector GET endpoints.

findings.summary: Agent key-value state and message history are persisted as world-readable plaintext JSON with no encryption or restrictive file mode.
findings.description: `KeyValueStore._save` writes the agent's state to `<name>_data.json` with `json.dump` and default `0644` permissions, and the same store backs message-history persistence when `store_message_history` is enabled. Message payloads and any developer-stored sensitive values therefore sit in cleartext on disk with no minimization, retrievable by any local user or process.

evidence.snippet: "_save opens the store file 'w' and json.dump()s all data with no encryption and no chmod (default 0644)"
"EnvelopeHistory(use_storage=store_message_history) persists message envelopes through the same plaintext KeyValueStore"

recommended_actions: Write the store file with `0600` permissions and offer an at-rest encryption option; document that message payloads persist in cleartext when `store_message_history` is enabled.

findings.summary: The `cosmpy` dependency is declared `>=0.12.1` with no upper bound, unlike the other framework dependencies which carry ceilings.
findings.description: `cosmpy` is the ledger/wallet client that signs on-chain transactions and holds key material, yet its version constraint has no upper cap, so a future major (potentially breaking or compromised) release satisfies the range on a fresh install. Every other runtime dependency is bounded with a ceiling and a `uv.lock` is committed, which makes this the one open supply-chain edge.

evidence.snippet: "\"cosmpy (>=0.12.1)\" — floor only, no upper bound, while peers use >=x,<y ceilings"

recommended_actions: Add an upper version bound to the `cosmpy` requirement (e.g. `>=0.12.1,<1.0`) consistent with the other pinned dependencies.

positives.title: Enforced, unit-tested envelope signature verification
positives.description: Inbound envelopes from agent senders are signature-verified against the claimed sender address before dispatch, and the SECP256k1 sign/verify path is covered by unit tests.

positives.title: Deterministic seed-derived identity
positives.description: Identity and wallet are derived deterministically from an operator seed, so a seeded agent's identity and wallet address survive restarts as the remit requires.

positives.title: Committed lockfile with version ceilings
positives.description: A `uv.lock` is committed and framework dependencies carry upper version bounds (except cosmpy), giving reproducible, provenance-known builds.

positives.title: Schema validation before dispatch
positives.description: Inbound message payloads and REST requests are validated against declared pydantic models before any handler runs, limiting malformed-input reach into handlers.

rows.purpose: framework runtime/info and warning messages incl envelope verification failures

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. The runtime has real, operative security primitives — SECP256k1 envelope signing with verification enforced for agent senders and covered by unit tests, deterministic seeded identity, pydantic schema validation before dispatch, and a committed `uv.lock` with version ceilings — which keep it off the floor in Zero Trust and lift Supply Chain to Established. But its default posture undercuts the trust model it promises: a public bind, a default-on inspector behind a spoofable loopback guard, absent replay/expiry enforcement, and plaintext key-at-rest. Monitoring is free-form stdout logging with no structured security-event trail, and there is no evidence of adversarial testing driving the design.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The runtime constrains inbound traffic to `/submit` plus registered typed handlers and reserved endpoints, a real surface limit, but `enable_agent_inspector=True` by default widens the surface with introspection, message-history, and registration-control endpoints the remit marks restricted.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Inbound payloads are validated against declared pydantic schemas before dispatch (`asgi.py` `Envelope.model_validate` and REST `request_model`), but persisted agent state and message history are written as world-readable plaintext JSON via `KeyValueStore._save` with no minimization or protection.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Envelope signature verification is genuinely implemented and enforced for agent senders (`asgi.py:363-365`), but the dispatch path never checks `expires`/`nonce` (replay), the inspector sits behind a spoofable `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` loopback guard on a `0.0.0.0` bind, sync responses are verified only when signed, and private keys are stored in cleartext.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies are range-pinned with upper ceilings, `uv.lock` is committed, provenance is clear (Fetch.ai / PyPI) and no secrets are hardcoded in framework source; the one gap is `cosmpy (>=0.12.1)` carrying no upper bound.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Functional unit tests exist including `test_msg_verify.py` and `test_server.py`, but there is no adversarial or red-team artifact and no evidence that security testing drove architectural change.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The framework logs via `get_logger` to stdout (and `uagents_core.log` in the core package) and warns on verification failure, but logging is free-form text with no structured, action-level security-event schema to support automated detection.
